params.title: About *One In Five* Cereals Grown Here Never Reaches A Plate.
params.kicker: The maths of farming blind — and what evidence buys back.

slides.alt_text: A farmer ploughs red soil with two white oxen under a storm-lit sky. Large yellow serif text reads: about one in five cereals grown here never reaches a plate.

stats.label: of cereals grown in Sub-Saharan Africa are lost between field and plate
estimated value of the region's post-harvest losses each year

slides.alt_text: A farmer ploughing with oxen at dusk. Two large statistics: about 20 percent of cereals in Sub-Saharan Africa are lost between field and plate, worth an estimated 4 billion US dollars a year. Source FAO and World Bank Missing Food, 2011.

params.eyebrow: The other side of the ledger
params.title:
Evidence Buys
Some Of It Back.
params.body:
Placing inputs where the field actually needs them is not a saving on the margins.

In trials, precision nitrogen management cut fertiliser use by *up to 30%* with no loss in yield — the same crop, for less money down.

slides.alt_text: A green tractor beside a tall maize stand. Text reads: evidence buys some of it back. Precision nitrogen management cut fertiliser use by up to 30 percent with no loss in yield.

params.title: Two Ways To Farm The Same Field.

bad.label: Farming blind
items: Trouble found on foot, after it shows

good.label: Reading the field
items: Inputs placed where the field asks for them
Every block read on its own terms
The decision made while it still counts

slides.alt_text: A farmer ploughing with oxen at dusk. A two-column comparison. Farming blind: inputs spread evenly, trouble found late, one number for twenty hectares, losses noticed at the weighbridge. Reading the field: inputs placed where needed, the sliding corner flagged early, every block read on its own terms, the decision made while it still counts.

params.question: What would a *fifth* of your harvest, back in the barn, change?
params.cta: SAVE THIS — SEND IT TO WHOEVER SIGNS OFF THE INPUT BUDGET

slides.alt_text: A young woman farmer in a straw hat leans on a wooden rail. Text asks: what would a fifth of your harvest, back in the barn, change? A yellow banner says save this and send it to whoever signs off the input budget.

caption:
Farming blind has a price, and it is not small.

Across Sub-Saharan Africa, roughly one in five cereals grown never makes it from the field to a plate — an estimated US$4 billion lost every year (FAO / World Bank, 'Missing Food', 2011). That is not one bad season. That is the baseline.

The encouraging half of the ledger: much of the waste is a decision problem, not a weather problem. Place inputs where the field actually needs them and the maths shifts fast — precision nitrogen trials cut fertiliser use by up to 30% with no loss in yield (CIMMYT, 2020). Same crop. Less money down.

That is the case for reading the field instead of guessing at it. Not a gadget — a cheaper, sharper way to spend what you were going to spend anyway.

Save this for your next input-budget conversation.
first_comment: Sources: the ~20% cereal loss share and the US$4bn annual figure are FAO / World Bank 'Missing Food' (2011); the 30% precision-nitrogen saving is CIMMYT (2020). Every number we post lives in our data bank with its source and year — we never invent stats.
rationale: The stakes post of the series. Hook states a sourced, felt loss in plain terms (one in five, never reaches a plate). Arc: quantify the loss with two sourced stats, then flip to what evidence recovers (sourced 30% input saving), then a blind-vs-reading comparison that frames precision as a spending decision rather than a gadget, closing on a save/send CTA aimed at the budget holder. Every stat carries a data-bank source; no fabricated results. British spelling, USD, Zimbabwe furniture (weighbridge, blocks, hectares, input budget).
